short_description: Mt. Fuji and Kawaguchiko Ohashi Bridge.
description: This cover captures a classic postcard view from the northern shore of Lake Kawaguchi. Mt. Fuji dominates the center-right of the design with its snow-capped silhouette. Kawaguchiko Ohashi Bridge (河口湖大橋), the concrete structure crossing the left side of the cover, spans Lake Kawaguchi and offers panoramic views of Mt. Fuji to travelers crossing the lake.

short_description: Geometric radial circle pattern manhole cover.
description: An unpainted, cast-iron manhole cover featuring an intricate geometric layout of concentric and interlocking circles radiating outwards from a central multi-pointed star or wheel motif. Includes a small rectangular text block on the left with the hiragana 'おすい' (osui), indicating wastewater.
notes: The text reads 'おすい' (Wastewater). The design is clean and abstract compared to regional pictorial covers.

short_description: Colorized pictorial cover featuring a Sika deer and cherry blossoms.
description: A beautifully detailed, colorized manhole cover depicting Nara's famous Sika deer standing amidst blooming cherry blossoms (sakura). The central crest features the traditional emblem of Nara City surrounded by a floral border. A small label at the bottom reads '汚水' (osui) for wastewater.
notes: Features the classic symbols of Nara: the sacred Sika deer and sakura. The text at the bottom reads '汚水' (Wastewater).

short_description: Rectangular fire hydrant utility lid with a diamond tread pattern.
description: A rectangular, dark iron utility lid built into a painted yellow frame. The lid face features a textured diamond grip pattern, two recessed lifting handles, and the Nara City emblem in the center. The text markings read '奈良市' (Nara City) and '消火栓' (Fire Hydrant). The painted frame above reads '駐車禁止' (No Parking).
notes: The orientation of the text on the lid reads '奈良市' (Nara City) on top and '消火栓' (Fire Hydrant) on the bottom. The curb stencil warns '駐車禁止' (No Parking).

short_description: Geometric patterned cover with a striking gold center emblem.
description: A circular manhole cover featuring a radial geometric pattern filled with a reddish-brown background paint. The standout feature is a highly detailed, golden-colored Nara City emblem in the exact center. The lower section includes the silver raised-text kanji '汚水' (osui), identifying it as a wastewater line.
notes: The central gold-painted insignia is the official city emblem of Nara. The lower text reads '汚水' (Wastewater).

description: This Uji City sewer cover features a stylized view of Uji Bridge, one of the city's best-known landmarks, framed by maple leaves and flowing cloud or river motifs. The design reflects Uji's historic riverside setting and its association with scenic walks, temple visits, and seasonal foliage. The inscription reads 宇治市, meaning Uji City, and おすい, meaning sewage.
notes: This appears to be a common Uji City design found in multiple locations around the city. The bridge motif is likely Uji Bridge over the Uji River.

short_description: Linear drain with Uji city flower
description: This decorative linear street drain in Uji features colored panels of yamabuki, or Japanese kerria, identified on the cover as the city's flower. The yellow blossoms and green leaves are set into a long drainage grate, adding a local botanical motif to an otherwise functional piece of street infrastructure. The central emblem appears to be the Uji City symbol, tying the design to the municipality.
notes: The inscription 市の花 やまぶき means city flower: yamabuki. Yamabuki is commonly known in English as Japanese kerria. This appears to be a decorative linear drain rather than a round manhole or utility cover.

short_description: Street drain with maple leaf inset
description: This rectangular Uji street drain combines a functional drainage grate with a small decorative inset panel showing maple leaves. The design is simpler than the larger round municipal covers, but it carries the same seasonal leaf motif seen around the city. The contrast between the utilitarian slotted grate and the small ornamental panel makes it a good example of how even everyday drainage infrastructure in Uji can include local visual identity.
notes: This type of rectangular drain was commonly found around Uji. The small inset panel appears to show maple leaves; the small text on the panel should be checked if a clearer close-up is available.

description: This brightly colored Uji utility cover marks access to firefighting water infrastructure. The design shows a red ladder fire truck on a yellow background, making the cover easy to identify in the street. The text 消火栓 means fire hydrant, 駐車禁止 means no parking, and the central 水 character means water.
notes: This design was found in multiple locations around Uji. It is a functional fire hydrant or firefighting water access cover rather than a decorative sewer cover.

short_description: Uji wastewater cover with leaf pattern
description: This Uji City sewer cover uses a repeating leaf pattern around the city emblem, creating a simple but distinctive municipal design. The leaf motif fits Uji's identity as a historic tea-growing and tea-culture center, while the overall design remains more abstract and functional than the city's larger scenic covers. The text 汚水 means sewage or wastewater.
notes: This design was commonly found around Uji. The central symbol appears to be the Uji City emblem. The repeated leaves may reference Uji's tea culture, but this should be treated as an interpretation rather than a confirmed design note.

description: This Uji wastewater cover has a distinctive three-tab outer shape and an open hexagonal grate pattern, giving it a more industrial appearance than the city's decorative scenic covers. A small central floral or municipal-style emblem sits above the label 汚水, meaning sewage or wastewater. The design is functional and geometric, but still includes a subtle decorative center detail.
notes: This cover appeared less common than the standard round Uji wastewater covers. The three-tab shape and open hexagonal grate pattern make it visually distinctive. The central symbol may be a floral or municipal-style emblem, but it should be confirmed before describing it more specifically.

short_description: Decorative entryway floor piece at Nakamura Tokichi
description: This octagonal metal floor piece was found just outside the door of the famous Nakamura Tokichi Main Branch in Uji. Unlike the municipal drain and utility covers in the collection, this appears to be a decorative architectural detail rather than a functional street cover. Its simple geometric form, dark metal finish, and raised circular studs give the entryway a textured, traditional-meets-industrial character.
notes: This is not a drain or utility cover. It was photographed outside Nakamura Tokichi Main Branch, a well-known tea shop in Uji.

description: This colorful Pokémon-themed decorative cover was found on the east side of Asagiri Bridge in Uji. The design features Scorbunny and Spinda among leafy greenery, with a pathway-like composition that fits the riverside walking area near the bridge. Like other Poké Lid-style covers, it turns a small piece of street infrastructure into a playful local landmark.
notes: One-off decorative Pokémon cover found on the east side of Asagiri Bridge. The Japanese names shown are ヒバニー and パッチール, corresponding to Scorbunny and Spinda.

description: This colorful Pokémon-themed cover was found at the Historical Park of Tea and Uji Town. The design features the tea-related Pokémon Sinistcha and Poltchageist, shown among matcha, tea utensils, maple leaves, and a traditional bridge-like backdrop. The cover connects Uji’s famous tea culture with Japan’s popular decorative utility cover tradition, turning a street feature into a playful local landmark.
notes: This appears to be a one-of-a-kind Pokémon decorative cover found at the Historical Park of Tea and Uji Town. The Japanese names shown are ヤバソチャ and チャデス, corresponding to Sinistcha and Poltchageist.

description: This small Uji City utility cover marks a water-service gate valve or shutoff valve. The blue color, compact round format, and central emblem distinguish it from the larger decorative sewer and storm drain covers found around the city. The text 仕切弁 means gate valve or shutoff valve, while 宇治市 identifies it as belonging to Uji City.
notes: The markings include 仕切弁, meaning gate valve or shutoff valve, and 宇治市, meaning Uji City. The numbers and letters, including 75 and S, may indicate pipe size, valve type, or maintenance classification.
